presents for preoperative exam. Patient is scheduled for a Left shoulder arthroscopy with
rotator cuff repair, subacromial decompression and arthroscopic biceps tenodesis on 4/10/26 at
Left Shoulder Rotator Cuff Tear with impingement, acromioclavicular arthritis, biceps tendinitis.
Patient fell September 22, 2025 and has had significant shoulder pain since then. He had a
cortisone injection that gave him a few days of relief. He has done 3 months of physical
therapy which only worsened his symptoms. At this point I have recommended surgery. We
will schedule the patient for left shoulder arthroscopy with rotator cuff repair, subacromial
decompression, distal clavicle excision, and arthroscopic biceps tenodesis.
Patient has a history of diabetes and his hemoglobin A1c was elevated because of a
medication miscommunication. He is resume that medication a month ago and is optimistic his
hemoglobin A1c is better. He has a follow-up tomorrow with his primary care provider. His wife
died a year ago. He is active and runs daily and wants to return to golf and bowling. He is a
Vietnam war veteran and was exposed to agent orange there.
Exam findings notable for the following:
Left shoulder exam: 90 degrees of active flexion. 4 out of 5 strength with resisted supraspinatus,
5 out of 5 with external rotation, 5 out of 5 with internal rotation. Positive Hawkins. Positive
O'Brien's. Positive cross body adduction. Tenderness at the acromioclavicular joint and biceps

MRI: left MRI Shoulder without contrast (imaging and outside report)
MRI of the left shoulder was reviewed, demonstrating the following findings: SDMI
1. Moderate supraspinatus tendinosis with low-to-moderate grade bursal-sided critical zone tear measuring 5 mm in AP
dimension. No full-thickness rotator cuff tear. Progressed since 2008 MRI.
2. Moderate infraspinatus tendinosis with bursal-sided tendon fraying. Progressed.
RW: Full-thickness tear at the insertion of the supraspinatus, impingement, biceps tendinitis.
Complete rotator cuff tear or rupture of left shoulder, not specified as traumatic (M75.122)
located on the left shoulder joint.
Associated diagnoses: Bicipital Tendinitis, AC Arthritis, and Shoulder Impingement
Indication: Rotator Cuff Tear, Complete, Left - M75.122, M75.22, M19.012, M75.42
Protocol: evaluate and treat per diagnosis/objective exam, left shoulder rotator cuff.
Recommend frequency of 2-3 times per week for 6-8 weeks.
- Therapeutic Exercises: All exercises pm per therapist, Home program exercises, Strengthening exercises, and
- Manual Therapy: All exercises prn per therapist.
- Modalities: All modalities prn per therapist.

VA Community Care Medical Policies describe standard VA health care benefit for services and procedures that community providers may recommend as
necessary for a Veteran. Prior to providing care, providers should use the Community Care Medical Policies as a reference when determining if a Veteran meets
VA clinical criteria. When additional services are requested, Community Care Medical Policies will be used to determine approval by a clinical reviewer.
Community Care Medical Policies & supporting Information can be found at:

VA Form 10-7080 - Approved Referral For Medical Care
VA Form 10-7080 - Approved Referral For Medical Care
The VA Order Reason for Request is the official clinical order. The scope of services and number of visits associated with
the medical care for this authorization can be found below. Necessary services that are not included must be requested
using the Request for Services procedures. Please visit the VHA Storefront www.va.gov/COMMUNITYCARE/providers/
index.asp for additional resources and requirements.
Procedural Overview - Standardized Episode of Care (SEOC)
This authorization covers services associated with the specialty(s) identified for this episode of care, including all medical
care listed below relevant to the referred care specified on the consult/referral order.
NOTE: Radiation therapy is NOT included in this episode of care approval. Radiation therapy requires a Request for
Services (RFS) with supporting documentation to allow for clinical review and consideration of a separate referral
NOTE: Pain neuromodulation devices for pain management should use the Pain Neuromodulation Devices Standardized
Initial outpatient evaluation, treatment, and follow-up visits for the referred condition
Diagnostic imaging relevant to the referred condition on the consult/referral order
Labs and pathology relevant to the referred condition on the consult/referral order
Diagnostic studies relevant to the referred condition on the consult/referral order including
but not limited to: electromyography (EMG), nerve conduction study (NCS)
Anesthesia consultation related to the procedure or surgery relevant to the referred
Pre-procedure medical and basic cardiac clearance provided by the specialist's office, as
clinically indicated (including H+P, labs, EKG, CXR, and echo when not done previously
at the VA within the required procedure timeframe) NOTE: cardiac testing or evaluation
outside of the above CXR, EKG, and echo, when clinically indicated, will require an RFS
Procedures performed by the orthopedic surgery provider relevant to the referred
condition on the consult/referral order including but not limited to: injections, biopsies,
Inpatient or observation admission for orthopedic procedure and/or procedure-related
complications, if indicated NOTE: Notify the referring VA of admission status to initiate and
facilitate care coordination and discharge planning

A Request for Services (RFS) is a provider-generated request for new or additional care outside the scope of the current
approved referral/authorization. Provider should always submit the RFS directly to the authorizing VAMC, preferably
via the HSRM portal. Providers should always submit an RFS on the same day it is determined it's needed and before
delivering care, unless it is emergent care. In that case, the RFS can be submitted simultaneously.
VA prefers providers submit an RFS via the HSRM portal, available on the VA's website
Go to the VA Storefront at https://www.va.gov/COMMUNITYCARE/providers/Care-Coordination.asp#RFS
Navigate to the link to the RFS form at the bottom of the section
Medical Records and documentation are required for all provider services. Providers are required to submit Medical
documentation directly to the authorizing VAMC, preferably via upload to HSRM.

The Standardized Episode of Care (SEOC) referral you have accepted does not include services that require third-party
VA Clinical Determinations and Indications (CDIs) describe standard VA health care benefits for services and procedures
that community providers may recommend as necessary for a Veteran. Prior to providing care, providers should use the
CDIs as a reference when determining if a Veteran meets VA clinical criteria. When additional services are requested,
CDIs will be used to determine approval by a clinical reviewer. CDIs and supporting information can be found at: https://
As part of furnishing the services authorized on the approved referral, you may recommend that medical devices,
adaptive equipment, or other items be provided for the treatment or rehabilitation of the Veteran's medical condition. In
such instances, you must complete a Request for Services (RFS) as outlined above.
The RFS must (1) indicate that the condition for which the item is being prescribed is within the scope of the authorized
services on the approved referral, (2) describe the item or service being prescribed with as much specificity as possible
(including manufacturer and model, needed custom measurements, size), and (3) provide a brief but thorough plain-
language explanation of how the item or service will serve the treatment or rehabilitation needs of the Veteran. All parts of
the RFS form must be filled out completely.
VA staff will determine whether the prescribed item or service is one that VA is authorized to purchase. If the item is not
something that we are authorized to purchase, VA staff will work with you to identify an alternative that will best meet the
Veteran's clinical needs. In making this determination, VA staff will consider your clinical judgment and expertise and work
with you to resolve outstanding issues quickly.
In the event of an urgent or emergent need, you may provide medical devices, necessary items or services prescribed
to Veterans receiving care in the community for urgent or emergent conditions at the time of healthcare service delivery
or soon thereafter. Urgent or emergent DME or Medical Devices may include, but are not limited to splints, crutches,
canes, slings, soft collars, walkers, and manual wheelchairs. DME dispensed directly to the Veteran based on an urgent
or emergent need will be paid by the CCN Third Party Administrator. Claims for these items should be submitted using the
process outlined in the Submitting Claims section of this form.

Clinical Information on the VA Order
Request follow-up, evaluation and treatment for 3-week history of left
shoulder pain status post mechanical fall. Patient reports pain at the
lateral deltoid, there is a soft tissue contusion noted, full range of
motion. Patient reports intermittent paresthesias to the left upper
extremity since the accident. He denies any previous surgery to the
extremity. Distal pulse intact. Brisk capillary refill distally.
Bilateral grip strength is equal and strong.
